title: How Toni Morrison Saw History
content:
The novelist Toni Morrison famously told a Princeton audience in 2017 that she did not like erasures of history. When asked about Confederate statues being torn down throughout the South, she surprisingly suggested leaving them up. Instead of removing them, she advocated for talking about the offense and putting another statue next to it to say the opposite. The audience laughed nervously, but she was completely serious about confronting history rather than hiding it. As a canonical American writer and Nobel laureate, she understood the deep power of historical artifacts.

Morrison had previously laid out her theory of cultural preservation in a brilliant essay titled "Rediscovering Black History." She recounted a story about the NAACP booking a hotel for a convention on the condition that two racist statuettes of Black jockeys be removed. The hotel management agreed, but before the statuettes could be removed, they were draped with sheets. Morrison's reaction was remarkable because she chided the NAACP for missing an opportunity to honor Black accomplishment. She believed it was better to mine an artifact for history than to conceal it for fear of white contempt.

She argued that instead of being delighted that the jockey profession once belonged to Black men, society hid their glory. By draping the figures, they ignored that 14 of the first 27 Kentucky Derby races were won by Black jockeys. This provocation was sheer Morrison, as part of her genius lay in discovering meaning where no one else imagined it could be found. She always believed in confronting the uncomfortable truths of the past rather than sweeping them away. This fearless approach to history is exactly what drove her to write her groundbreaking first novel, The Bluest Eye.
